answers.question: List of scenarios
answers.evidence: We analysed three different scenarios: 'ambitious and orderly', which assumed the conditions under SSP1-1.9 (average warming of 1.4 degrees C by 2100); 'delayed and disorderly', which assumed the conditions under SSP2-4.5 (average warming of 2.7 degrees C by 2100); and 'hothouse world', which assumed the conditions under SSP3-7.0 (average warming of 3.6 degrees by 2100).
answers.notes: Scenario names and their SSP identifiers are explicitly stated.

answers.question: Global temperature outcome by 2100
answers.answer: Three temperature outcomes by 2100 are described: 1.4°C (SSP1-1.9, Ambitious and orderly), 2.7°C (SSP2-4.5, Delayed/Disorderly), and 3.6°C (SSP3-7.0, Hothouse world). Mid-century (2040–2060) temperatures are also given: 1.6°C, 2.0°C, and 2.1°C respectively.
answers.location_hint: Table 1 / Table 2 in Appendix 1: Climate Resilience, p.113
answers.notes: Temperature outcomes are explicitly tabulated for both mid-century and end-of-century periods.

answers.question: Scenario provider
answers.answer: Yes. The scenario provider is Tonkin & Taylor (external consultants), working in collaboration with AFT and a Technical Working Group (TWG) for the healthcare sector. The approach was based on guidance from the Taskforce for Climate Related Financial Disclosures (TCFD) and the New Zealand External Reporting Board (XRB).
answers.evidence: In August 2023, our Climate Risk Working Group engaged external consultants to support our scenario analysis. In collaboration with Tonkin & Taylor, AFT contributed to the Technical Working Group supporting the development of the healthcare sector-level climate scenarios. The approach to developing the scenarios was based on guidance from the Taskforce for Climate Related Financial Disclosures (TCFD) and the New Zealand External Reporting Board (XRB).
answers.notes: Tonkin & Taylor is explicitly named as the external consultant. TCFD and XRB are named as the guidance frameworks used.

answers.question: Pick-and-mix of SSPs and RCPs
answers.evidence: We analysed three different scenarios: 'ambitious and orderly', which assumed the conditions under SSP1-1.9 (average warming of 1.4 degrees C by 2100); 'delayed and disorderly', which assumed the conditions under SSP2-4.5 (average warming of 2.7 degrees C by 2100); and 'hothouse world', which assumed the conditions under SSP3-7.0 (average warming of 3.6 degrees by 2100).
answers.notes: Only SSP identifiers are used (SSP1-1.9, SSP2-4.5, SSP3-7.0). No RCP identifiers are mentioned anywhere in the document. There is no mixing of scenario families.

answers.question: Customised scenarios
answers.evidence: In collaboration with Tonkin & Taylor, AFT contributed to the Technical Working Group supporting the development of the healthcare sector-level climate scenarios... AFT used the three scenarios developed at the sector level for its entity-level scenario assessment, focusing on both the global and New Zealand-specific parameters in line with the scope of its operations, manufacturing, distribution networks and end users.
answers.notes: The scenarios were developed specifically for the healthcare sector (not used off-the-shelf) and further adapted to include New Zealand-specific parameters for AFT's entity-level assessment.

answers.question: Co-produced climate scenarios
answers.evidence: In collaboration with Tonkin & Taylor, AFT contributed to the Technical Working Group supporting the development of the healthcare sector-level climate scenarios. The approach to developing the scenarios was based on guidance from the Taskforce for Climate Related Financial Disclosures (TCFD) and the New Zealand External Reporting Board (XRB). Six steps were undertaken, as shown in Figure 5, with a series of workshops being held with the TWG as the project progressed.
answers.notes: AFT participated in a Technical Working Group (TWG) with Tonkin & Taylor and other healthcare sector entities, with a series of collaborative workshops. This constitutes participatory/co-produced scenario development.

answers.question: Baseline scenarios
answers.evidence: We analysed three different scenarios: 'ambitious and orderly', which assumed the conditions under SSP1-1.9 (average warming of 1.4 degrees C by 2100); 'delayed and disorderly', which assumed the conditions under SSP2-4.5 (average warming of 2.7 degrees C by 2100); and 'hothouse world', which assumed the conditions under SSP3-7.0 (average warming of 3.6 degrees by 2100).
answers.notes: The document describes three climate scenarios but does not identify any of them as a "baseline" or "reference" scenario against which the others are compared. No business-as-usual baseline is explicitly defined.

answers.question: Model used
answers.evidence: All emissions were calculated using Toitū emanage with emissions factors and Global Warming Potentials provided by the Programme.
answers.location_hint: Table 9: AFT's GHG reporting methodology, Appendix 1, p.118
answers.notes: Toitū emanage is named as the tool used for GHG emissions calculations, not a climate or IAM model. No climate models (e.g. GCMs, IAMs) are explicitly named in the scenario analysis section. The document references SSP scenarios (which originate from IPCC/IAM work) but does not name any specific climate or integrated assessment model used in the scenario analysis itself.

answers.question: Damage from acute physical risk considered
answers.evidence: Physical risk: In a hothouse scenario, increased severity of extreme weather resulting in interruption to manufacturing/increased interruptions /outages to freighting and damage to/loss of sites... The business activity is subject to the most acute risk if severe weather interrupts the manufacture or distribution of products.
answers.location_hint: Appendix 1: Climate Resilience, p.115 and p.119
answers.notes: The document identifies acute physical risks (extreme weather events causing damage/loss of sites, manufacturing interruptions, freighting outages) and rates their likelihood and consequence across scenarios and time horizons. However, quantified financial loss estimates are not yet provided — the document states "further work in FY25 will investigate the financial impact." The risks are identified and rated qualitatively (medium/high) but not yet monetised.

answers.question: Non-temperature variables in scenarios
answers.evidence: Carbon price... Global GDP per capita by 2050... NZ GDP impact... NZ population... Percentage NZ >65... Financial impact of supply chain disruptions... Policy reaction to climate change... Regional policy variation... Socioeconomic context... [and from Table 4/5:] Changes to mean temp, weather patterns... Access to water for production... sea level rise, frequent extreme weather events, and permanent changes in temperature patterns.
answers.notes: Multiple non-temperature variables are used across the scenario narratives and risk tables.

answers.question: Scale of scenarios
answers.evidence: AFT used the three scenarios developed at the sector level for its entity-level scenario assessment, focusing on both the global and New Zealand-specific parameters in line with the scope of its operations, manufacturing, distribution networks and end users.
answers.notes: The scenarios incorporate both global parameters (SSP-based global warming trajectories) and New Zealand-specific parameters (NZ GDP, NZ population, NZ policy context).

answers.question: Time frames
answers.answer: Four time horizons are defined: Short-term (less than 1 year), Medium-term (1–3 years), Long-term (3–10 years), and Very Long-term (11–75 years). Rationales are explicitly provided for each, aligned with business planning cycles and scenario timeframes.
answers.evidence: Short-term: Less than 1 year (aligned with stock planning and budget cycles)... Medium-term: Next 1-3 years (aligned with supplier and manufacturing management)... Long-term: Next 3–10 years (aligned with AFT strategy and product development cycles)... Very Long-term: Next 11–75 years (aligned with scenario timeframes)... The Healthcare sector-level scenarios' short, medium and long term time horizons spanned to 2100, to align with projected climate physical impacts over this period. Our entity level time horizons align with our strategic planning horizons and financial decision-making.
answers.location_hint: Table 2: Time horizons, Appendix 1: Climate Resilience, p.114
answers.notes: Rationales are explicitly provided for each time horizon, linking them to business planning cycles (stock/budget, supplier/manufacturing management, strategy/product development, and scenario timeframes).

answers.question: Justification for scenario/model choice
answers.evidence: The approach to developing the scenarios was based on guidance from the Taskforce for Climate Related Financial Disclosures (TCFD) and the New Zealand External Reporting Board (XRB). Six steps were undertaken, as shown in Figure 5, with a series of workshops being held with the TWG as the project progressed.
answers.notes: The justification is framed in terms of compliance with TCFD and XRB guidance, and the use of a structured six-step methodology with a Technical Working Group. However, there is no explicit statement explaining why SSP1-1.9, SSP2-4.5, and SSP3-7.0 were chosen over other SSP scenarios (e.g. SSP5-8.5). The justification is process-based rather than scenario-specific.
